items.q: What does Spearman's rank correlation coefficient measure?
items.correct: The strength and direction of a relationship between two ranked variables. Values range from -1 (perfect negative) through 0 (no correlation) to +1 (perfect positive).
items.misconception: A Spearman value close to 1 proves that one variable causes the other.
items.random_wrong: Spearman's rank measures the average rank of all the data points.
items.why: Spearman shows correlation strength, not causation. Significance testing is also needed to check the result is unlikely by chance — students report rs values without context or significance level.

items.q: What is the difference between a megacity and a world city?
items.correct: A megacity is defined by population (over 10 million); a world city is defined by global economic and cultural influence (e.g. New York, London) — so a city can be one without the other.
items.misconception: Megacities and world cities are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: A megacity has over 1 million people and a world city has over 5 million.
items.why: Dhaka and Kinshasa are megacities but not world cities; Zurich is a world city but not a megacity. The distinction is quantitative (size) vs qualitative (function and global connections).

items.q: What is the difference between push and pull factors in migration?
items.correct: Push factors drive people away from their origin (war, poverty, persecution, disaster); pull factors attract people to a destination (jobs, safety, family, healthcare, education).
items.misconception: Push and pull factors are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Push factors are physical and pull factors are economic.
items.why: Most migration involves both — people leave AND are attracted somewhere. Pure push (refugees) or pure pull (job offers) is rare. The framework helps but oversimplifies real decisions.

items.q: What is the main driver of Amazon deforestation?
items.why: It's Cattle ranching. “Mining” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Emergent. “Canopy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Roughly what percentage of the Amazon has been lost since 1970?
items.why: Exact answer: 17 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
